name: DBMS
description: Relational data modeling, SQL, normalization, transactions, and query processing concepts.

units.title: Introduction and relational model
units.summary: Databases vs files, tables, keys, and integrity.

content.value: A Database Management System (DBMS) provides centralized storage, concurrency control, recovery, and declarative querying. Applications interact through a well-defined API—often SQL—rather than managing files manually.
The relational model represents data as relations (tables) with named attributes (columns). Each row is a tuple; keys identify rows uniquely.

content.code:
INSERT INTO student (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Ada');
SELECT * FROM student WHERE id = 1;

content.value: Primary keys uniquely identify rows; foreign keys reference other tables, enforcing referential integrity. Constraints are enforced by the DBMS on insert/update/delete.
Candidate keys are minimal superkeys; you choose one as primary. Alternate keys may be declared UNIQUE.

content.code: ALTER TABLE student ADD CONSTRAINT uq_email UNIQUE (email);

mcqs.question: In the relational model, a table is also called a:
Which constraint enforces parent/child relationships across tables?

theory.question: What problems does using a DBMS solve compared to ad-hoc files?
theory.answer: Centralized integrity, concurrent access with isolation, crash recovery, declarative querying, security and access control, and reduced duplication of parsing/validation logic in each application.

coding.title: Create and query
coding.description: Write SQL to create table course(id, title), insert two rows, and select titles ordered alphabetically.
coding.hint: Use CREATE TABLE, INSERT, SELECT ... ORDER BY.

starterCode.code:
-- SQLite-style example
-- TODO: CREATE, INSERT x2, SELECT ORDER BY title

content.value: WHERE filters rows before aggregation; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY. ORDER BY controls presentation order—use indexes thoughtfully for large tables.

content.code:
SELECT name, score
FROM student
WHERE score >= 60
ORDER BY score DESC, name ASC;

content.value: NULL comparisons need IS NULL / IS NOT NULL—equality to NULL is unknown, not true.

content.code: SELECT * FROM student WHERE advisor IS NULL;

content.value: INNER JOIN keeps only matching pairs; LEFT OUTER JOIN preserves left rows even without a match (right side columns become NULL).

content.code:
SELECT s.name, c.title
FROM student s
JOIN enroll e ON s.id = e.sid
JOIN course c ON c.id = e.cid;

content.value: Join order and predicates affect performance; optimizers reorder joins using statistics.

content.code:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM enroll e
LEFT JOIN student s ON s.id = e.sid
WHERE s.id IS NULL; -- orphans

mcqs.question: Which clause filters rows before grouping?
Which join keeps all rows from the left table?

theory.question: Difference between WHERE and HAVING?
theory.answer: WHERE filters individual rows before aggregation. HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY, and may reference aggregates like COUNT(*) or SUM(amount).

coding.title: Aggregation
coding.description: Given sales(order_id, amount), write SQL to return each day’s total amount (assume a date column exists).

starterCode.code: -- SELECT day, SUM(amount) ... GROUP BY day

content.value: A functional dependency X → Y holds if any two tuples agreeing on X also agree on Y. Dependencies guide decomposition to reduce redundancy and anomalies.

content.code:
-- Anomalies example: repeating groups in one wide table
-- Split into student(id, name) and phone(student_id, number)

content.value: Armstrong’s axioms help infer dependencies; closure of attribute sets finds keys.

content.code: -- Normalization is schema design; SQL shows resulting structure

content.value: 1NF eliminates repeating groups; 2NF removes partial dependencies on composite keys; 3NF removes transitive dependencies on non-key attributes. BCNF tightens determinants to superkeys.
Denormalization is sometimes intentional for read-heavy workloads—measure before trading integrity for speed.

content.code: -- Materialized views cache denormalized results

mcqs.question: Which normal form removes partial dependencies on part of a composite key?
Redundant storage of derivable data often causes:

theory.question: What is BCNF in one sentence?
theory.answer: Every determinant must be a superkey: for every nontrivial FD X → Y, X must be a superkey of the relation.

coding.title: Decompose a table
coding.description: Given R(emp_id, emp_name, dept_id, dept_name) with FDs emp_id → emp_name, dept_id → dept_name, propose 3NF schemas.
coding.hint: Separate employee and department; link with dept_id FK.

units.title: Transactions
units.summary: ACID, isolation levels, and recovery basics.

content.value: Atomicity groups operations into all-or-nothing units. Consistency preserves declared rules. Isolation hides concurrent transactions from each other according to level. Durability commits survive crashes.
ROLLBACK aborts a transaction, undoing its effects if supported by the engine’s logging.
READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads; READ COMMITTED avoids them; REPEATABLE READ stabilizes reads; SERIALIZABLE prevents phantoms—at higher cost.

content.code: SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

content.value: MVCC engines often implement snapshot isolation with different anomaly profiles than lock-based serializability.

content.code: -- Check your DB docs: isolation names vary slightly

mcqs.question: Which ACID property ensures committed writes survive crashes?

theory.question: What is a schedule and why serializability matters?
theory.answer: A schedule orders operations from concurrent transactions. Conflict serializability means the schedule is equivalent to some serial order, preserving isolation guarantees users reason about.

coding.title: Transfer funds safely
coding.description: Sketch SQL for transferring amount between two rows in accounts with constraints (non-negative balance).
coding.hint: Use a transaction; consider SELECT ... FOR UPDATE if locking.

starterCode.code:
BEGIN;
-- TODO: lock rows, check balances, update both, COMMIT

content.value: Indexes trade write overhead and storage for faster lookups. B+-trees are common for range queries; hash indexes help equality lookups.

content.code:
CREATE INDEX idx_student_name ON student(name);
EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT * FROM student WHERE name = 'Ada';

content.value: Covering indexes include all selected columns to avoid table lookups—design with query patterns in mind.

content.code: CREATE INDEX idx_cover ON orders(customer_id) INCLUDE (order_date, total);

content.value: Optimizers rewrite queries, estimate cardinalities, pick join orders, and choose access paths using statistics. Outdated stats cause bad plans.

content.code: ANALYZE student; -- refresh statistics (syntax varies)

content.value: Understanding EXPLAIN output is essential for tuning—look for sequential scans on large tables.

content.code: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) SELECT ...

mcqs.question: Which structure is widely used for range scans on indexed columns?
Why might an optimizer choose a sequential scan?

theory.question: What is selectivity?
theory.answer: The fraction of rows matching a predicate. Highly selective predicates benefit from indexes; low selectivity may favor full scans.

coding.title: Index for a query
coding.description: For WHERE status = ? AND created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?, suggest a composite index order and explain briefly.
coding.hint: Equality column first, then range column.

units.title: NoSQL overview and design trade-offs
units.summary: When document/key-value stores help—and what you give up.

content.value: NoSQL systems relax parts of the relational model for scale, flexible schemas, or specialized access patterns. Choose based on consistency needs, query shapes, and operational maturity.

content.code:
-- Relational remains excellent for many workloads
-- Document DB example is API-driven, not SQL standard

content.value: CAP theorem reminds you: during partitions, pick between availability and consistency; real systems offer tunable knobs.

content.code: -- Design documents: embed vs reference

content.value: Model around access patterns: read-heavy feeds favor denormalization and caching; write-heavy telemetry may prefer append-only logs and batch compaction.

content.code: -- Star/snowflake schemas for analytics warehouses

content.value: Always measure: synthetic benchmarks rarely match production skew and concurrency.

content.code: -- End of DBMS track sample queries

mcqs.question: Which is a common motivation for document databases?
Eventual consistency means:
options: Replicas converge over time without partitions

theory.question: Name one trade-off when moving from RDBMS to a key-value store.
theory.answer: You may lose declarative joins and rich constraints, pushing more integrity logic into applications and complicating ad-hoc querying.

coding.title: Schema choice
coding.description: Compare storing user profiles as JSON documents vs normalized tables for a read-mostly social feed.
coding.hint: Think about update frequency, query patterns, and integrity.
